senses.definition: An old or rare spelling meaning practices or actions, especially schemes or methods.
examples: The historian wrote about the dark practicks of the royal court.
Such practicks will not be tolerated in this company.

senses.definition: A local ship’s pilot in some Spanish‑speaking ports, especially in older or specialized maritime use.
examples: The captain waited for the practico to guide the ship into the harbor.
Without a practico, navigating the narrow channel was too risky.

senses.definition: An old plural form meaning practices, ways of doing things, or methods, especially in a professional or technical area.
examples: Medical practics have changed greatly over the last century.
The book compares the practics of different legal systems.

senses.definition: A supervised practical course where students apply the theory they have learned, especially in teaching, medicine, or social work.
examples: She completed her teaching practicum at a local primary school.
The nursing program includes a hospital practicum in the final year.

senses.definition: Plural of practicum: supervised practical courses where students apply the theory they have learned.
examples: The degree requires two practicums in local schools.
Many students find their practicums more challenging than lectures.

senses.definition: An old or specialized spelling of pratique, meaning official permission for a ship to enter a port after showing it is free from contagious disease.
examples: The vessel received practique after the health inspection.
Without practique, the ship had to remain outside the harbor.

senses.definition: Plural of practique: permissions allowing ships to enter port after health checks.
examples: Several ships waited offshore, hoping for their practiques.
The authorities refused practiques because of the epidemic.

senses.definition: An old or rare word meaning someone who practises a religion or profession.
examples: As a devout practisant, he attended services every week.
Each practisant must follow the code of ethics.

senses.definition: Plural of practisant: people who actively follow a religion or practise a profession.
examples: The practisants gathered for the evening ceremony.
All practisants must register with the council.

senses.definition: To do an activity again and again to become better at it.
examples: You need to practise speaking English every day.
She practises the piano for two hours each evening.

senses.definition: To work in a job that needs special training, especially as a doctor or lawyer.
examples: He has practised as a lawyer for twenty years.
She plans to practise medicine in her home country.

senses.definition: To do something regularly because of your beliefs, rules, or habits.
examples: They are free to practise their religion.
The company does not practise discrimination.

senses.definition: Experienced and skilled at doing something because you have done it many times.
examples: With a practised hand, she folded the paper into a crane.
He spoke with the confidence of a practised public speaker.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of practise: did an activity repeatedly to improve, or worked in a trained profession.
examples: She practised the song until she knew it by heart.
He practised as a surgeon for thirty years.

senses.definition: Someone who regularly does a particular activity, especially a religion, belief, or skill.
examples: She is a regular practiser of meditation.
As a practiser of the faith, he follows strict rules.

senses.definition: People who regularly do a particular activity, especially a religion, belief, or skill.
examples: The temple was full of practisers from many countries.
Dedicated practisers often meet early in the morning.

senses.definition: Third person singular of practise: does an activity again and again to become better at it.
examples: She practises the violin every afternoon.
He practises speaking English with his friends.

senses.definition: Third person singular of practise: works in a job that needs special training.
examples: She practises as a dentist in the city.
He currently practises law in London.

senses.definition: Actively working in a job that needs special training, such as a doctor or lawyer.
examples: She is a practising lawyer with fifteen years of experience.
Only practising doctors can prescribe this medicine.

senses.definition: Actively following a religion or belief in daily life.
examples: He is a practising Catholic.
Although raised in the faith, she is not a practising believer.

senses.definition: Present participle of practise: doing an activity again and again to become better, or working in a trained profession.
examples: She is practising her presentation for tomorrow.
He is currently practising as a surgeon in Paris.

senses.definition: Relating to practical work or professional practice, in very rare or old use.
examples: His practitional experience in law was highly respected.
The report focused on the practitional side of the profession.

senses.definition: A person who works in a skilled job, especially as a doctor or lawyer.
examples: You should consult a medical practitioner about these symptoms.
She is a leading legal practitioner in this field.

senses.definition: Someone who regularly does or uses a particular activity, method, or belief.
examples: She is a skilled practitioner of modern dance.
He is a serious practitioner of mindfulness techniques.

senses.definition: Plural of practitioner: people who work in skilled jobs, especially doctors or lawyers.
examples: Medical practitioners must renew their licenses regularly.
Many legal practitioners attended the conference.

senses.definition: Plural of practitioner: people who regularly do or use a particular activity, method, or belief.
examples: Many practitioners of alternative medicine work in this clinic.
Experienced practitioners of the art shared their techniques.

senses.definition: Relating to practitioners or professional practice, in very rare or old use.
examples: He wrote about the practictionery aspects of medical ethics.
Her practictionery duties left her little free time.

senses.definition: An extremely rare form related to practice, sometimes used to mean practical or practising.
examples: The course includes both theory and practive elements.
His practive skills were stronger than his theoretical knowledge.

senses.definition: A beta‑blocker drug that was used to treat irregular heartbeats but is now little used because of serious side effects.
examples: Practolol was once prescribed for certain heart rhythm problems.
Because of its risks, practolol has largely been replaced by newer drugs.

senses.definition: Plural of practolol: doses or types of the beta‑blocker drug practolol.
examples: Older studies compared practolols with other beta‑blockers.
The report listed several practolols and their effects.

senses.definition: In some technical fields, a shortened form of picoradian, a unit for very small angles, used rarely.
examples: The beam was aligned within a few prad of the target angle.
Such measurements at the prad level require advanced instruments.

senses.definition: A male given name, common in India and other South Asian countries.
examples: Pradeep moved to Canada to study engineering.
I met Pradeep at the conference last year.

senses.definition: In Indian philosophy, especially Sāṃkhya, the original or primal matter from which the universe develops.
examples: In Sāṃkhya thought, pradhana is the root cause of the material world.
The text describes pradhana as uncreated and eternal.

senses.definition: Short for the Prado Museum, a famous art museum in Madrid, Spain.
examples: We spent the afternoon at the Prado.
The Prado has an impressive collection of Spanish paintings.

senses.definition: A family name found in Spanish‑speaking countries.
examples: Mr. Prado welcomed us into his office.
The novel was written by Ana Prado.

senses.definition: A very informal spelling sometimes used online as a short form of people or things connected with the Prada fashion brand.
examples: All the fashion prads were at the launch party.
She showed off her new prads on social media.

senses.definition: The front part of the abdomen in some animals, especially scorpions and certain insects.
examples: The praeabdomen of the scorpion is clearly wider than the tail.
Researchers measured the length of the praeabdomen in each specimen.

senses.definition: Located in front of the acetabulum (hip socket), used especially in descriptive anatomy.
examples: The fossil shows a distinct praeacetabular crest on the ilium.
Praeacetabular structures are important in classifying dinosaur hips.

senses.definition: An old spelling of preamble, meaning an introduction to a law, speech, or written document.
examples: The charter’s praeamble sets out the basic principles.
After a short praeamble, the agreement lists the main terms.

senses.definition: Plural of praeamble: introductory parts of laws, speeches, or documents.
examples: The treaties have long, complex praeambles.
Different praeambles express the values of each nation.

senses.definition: Located in front of the anus, used in technical descriptions of animal anatomy.
examples: The fish has a distinctive praeanal fin fold.
Praeanal scales were counted to help identify the species.

senses.definition: An old term for the superior vena cava, the large vein that carries blood to the heart from the upper body.
examples: The surgeon inspected the praecava for any blockage.
Damage to the praecava can be life‑threatening.

senses.definition: A formal written request to a court asking it to take a particular action, such as setting a date or entering a judgment.
examples: The lawyer filed a praecipe for a hearing date.
A praecipe was submitted to enter the default judgment.

senses.definition: Plural of praecipe: formal written requests asking a court to take certain actions.
examples: Several praecipes were filed before the hearing.
The judge reviewed the praecipes for accuracy.

senses.definition: A rare Latin‑based form related to precipitation, especially in old scientific or medical texts.
examples: The alchemist described the praecipitatio of the substance in detail.
In older writings, praecipitatio may refer to chemical settling.

senses.definition: A rare Latin‑based term meaning the chief thing or special share, especially in old legal or scholarly writing.
examples: The eldest son received the praecipuum of the estate.
In this system, a praecipuum is granted to the main heir.

senses.definition: A very rare form meaning early developing or precocious, especially in scientific Latin‑style usage.
examples: The author described certain plants as praecoces.
Praecoces species flower before the others.

senses.definition: An older spelling of precocial, describing young animals that are active and able to move around soon after birth.
examples: Ducks are praecocial birds whose chicks can walk soon after hatching.
In praecocial species, the young require less parental care.

senses.definition: Something known in advance, especially in philosophical discussions of knowledge or in parapsychology.
examples: The philosopher questioned whether any praecognitum is truly certain.
In the report, the vision was treated as a praecognitum of the event.

senses.definition: A bone or part of a bone in the shoulder region of some vertebrates, lying in front of the coracoid.
examples: The fossil’s praecoracoid is well preserved.
Birds show a reduced praecoracoid compared with reptiles.

senses.definition: The area in front of the heart and stomach, or the internal organs there, in old medical use.
examples: The physician noted a tightness in the praecordia.
In older texts, pains in the praecordia often meant heart trouble.

senses.definition: Relating to the area in front of the heart and stomach.
examples: The patient complained of praecordial pain.
Praecordial sounds were heard during the examination.

senses.definition: The front of the chest over the heart and upper stomach.
examples: A bruise was visible over the praecordium.
The doctor placed the stethoscope on the praecordium.

senses.definition: A rare anatomical term for a front horn‑like part, such as a part of the brain or spinal cord.
examples: Damage to the praecornu affected limb movement.
The researcher identified changes in the praecornu of the spinal cord.

senses.definition: Early or premature in development, used in old medical and botanical terms like dementia praecox.
examples: The doctor once used the term dementia praecox for certain mental illnesses.
In botany, praecox may describe plants that flower early.

senses.definition: A part of the parietal lobe on the inner surface of the brain, involved in complex tasks such as memory and self‑related thinking.
examples: Brain scans showed increased activity in the praecuneus.
The praecuneus is important in many higher cognitive functions.

senses.definition: Relating to land or landed property, especially in legal or historical contexts.
examples: The tenant enjoyed certain praedial rights.
Praedial servitudes were attached to the estate.

senses.definition: In historical use, someone who holds or enjoys praedial rights or property, such as a tenant bound to the land.
examples: In some systems, the praedialist owed labor to the landowner.
The law distinguished between free citizens and praedialists.

senses.definition: Rights or duties connected with land or landed property, mentioned in historical legal writing.
examples: The charter listed several praedialities attached to the estate.
Praedialities often passed from one generation to the next.
